unitTitle: Y7 Modelling data with spreadsheets (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Getting to know a spreadsheet — rows, columns, cells & formatting
lessons.objectives:
I can name the rows, columns and cells in a spreadsheet
I can write a cell reference like B3
I can select a range of cells like A1:F5
I can change a cell's fill colour and borders
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: data · cell · cell reference · row · column · range · select. TA: everyone has the spreadsheet app open. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — Olympic medals (starter worksheet). Pupils TICK who won the most gold (no writing). Draw out that a list is awkward and a grid is easier.
   • Support: tick that the list is “data”.  • Core: say why the list is awkward.  • Challenge: how a spreadsheet would help.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — rows and columns. Columns = letters across the top, rows = numbers down the side. A cell reference is the column letter then the row number (B3). Likely error: writes 3B → fix-words “letter first, then number.”
3. (20–30) You-do — cell-reference bingo (slide image). Teacher calls a cell (e.g. B3); pupils find it on their bingo sheet.
   • Support: point to the column then slide down to the row.  • Core: find cells unaided.  • Challenge: a pupil calls cells for the class.
4. (30–48) Pixel-art flags (activity worksheet). Select a range (A1:F5), change fill colour, add borders, to build a flag. Keep a screenshot of the finished flag.
   • Support: a printed “colour THESE cells” strip.  • Core: follow the range list.  • Challenge: add a border and change its thickness. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (48–55) Plenary — pupils self-tick the four “I can…”; show their flag. TA notes who selected a range and changed fill colour unaided.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Quick calculations — formulas & autofill
lessons.objectives:
I can write a formula that starts with =
I can use cell references in a formula
I can use the operators + - * /
I can use the autofill tool to fill cells quickly
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: drag handle · autofill · formula · cell reference. TA: each pupil has their own copy of the activities spreadsheet. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — times tables (starter worksheet). Pupils CHOOSE the symbol a formula starts with (=) and the symbols for × and ÷. Draw out: every formula starts with =.
   • Support: tick * and /.  • Core: fill the blank operator.  • Challenge: why type a formula at all.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — a formula with cell references (= C20*D20). Press Enter. Likely error: forgets the = → fix-words “start with equals.” We-do: predict the next formula (= C21*D21).
3. (25–40) You-do — autofill. Select 2–3 cells, drag the handle down to fill the rest; combine autofill with a formula to fill the Answer column.
   • Support: a printed “drag from here” strip.  • Core: autofill the pattern.  • Challenge: autofill the formula column too.
4. (40–50) Party time — double a recipe’s quantities with a formula (=B2*2), then autofill. Keep a screenshot of the finished times table (activity worksheet).
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; one-minute calculation check. TA notes who used autofill with a formula.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Collecting data — data, information & sources
lessons.objectives:
I can write a formula using the correct symbols
I can explain the difference between data and information
I can explain the difference between a primary and a secondary source
I can help collect data with a survey
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: data · information · source · primary source · secondary source. TA: starter worksheet printed/shared. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — formulas recap (starter worksheet). Pupils write the FORMULA, not the answer (= A1 * C1). Fill the blank operator. Recap from last lesson.
2. (10–22) I-do — data vs information. Data = raw facts and figures; information = data organised so it has meaning. Then primary source (you collect it) vs secondary source (someone else did).
   • Support: tick that a class survey is a primary source.  • Core: explain data vs information.  • Challenge: classify a website table as primary/secondary and justify.
3. (22–45) We-do/you-do — design a class survey (activity worksheet). As a class, brainstorm questions (no names/personal details); each pupil writes one survey question. Save the responses spreadsheet for later lessons.
   • Support: choose a question from a short list.  • Core: write a sensible question.  • Challenge: write a question that gives numeric data to analyse.
4. (45–52) Plenary — word-match (matching question on the activity worksheet): match data/information/primary/secondary to their meanings.
5. (52–55) Self-tick the four “I can…”. TA notes who can explain primary vs secondary.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; survey stays anonymous (no pupil details collected).

lessons.title: Become a data master — functions & charts
lessons.objectives:
I can use SUM to add a range
I can use MAX and MIN to find the biggest and smallest
I can use COUNTA to count non-blank cells
I can create a chart from data
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: chart · pie chart · bar chart · function · maximum · minimum. TA: each pupil has their own copy of the data spreadsheet. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — cake vote (starter worksheet). Pupils type initials under a treat in a shared sheet; a pie chart updates. CHOOSE what a pie chart helps you do.
   • Support: tick SUM as the “add a range” function.  • Core: why a chart helps.  • Challenge: name the function that counts non-blank cells (COUNTA).
2. (10–30) I-do → we-do — four functions. = SUM(B2:B10) adds; = MAX / = MIN find biggest/smallest; = COUNTA counts non-blank cells. Likely error: forgets the brackets/range → fix-words “function name, then the range in brackets.”
3. (30–45) You-do — try each function on your own copy of the class data; then insert a chart (pie + bar). Keep a screenshot of a chart (activity worksheet).
   • Support: choose the data range together.  • Core: one function + one chart.  • Challenge: total/biggest/smallest of donations and a labelled chart.
4. (45–52) Plenary — function quiz (single-choice questions on the activity worksheet).
5. (52–55) Self-tick the four “I can…”. TA notes who used a function and made a chart unaided.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

lessons.title: Level up your data skills — COUNTIF, AVERAGE, IF, sort & filter
lessons.objectives:
I can sort and filter a data set
I can use COUNTIF to count cells that match a rule
I can use AVERAGE to find the mean
I can use IF to show different text
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: header · filter · average · criteria · condition. TA: each pupil has their own copy of the larger data set. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — functions warm-up (starter worksheet). Pupils CHOOSE MAX and COUNTA; write a cell-reference range (B1:B10) in the blank. Recap from last lesson.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — sort with headers (keep the header row on top; likely error: header sorted into the data → fix-words “tell it my data has headers”) and filter (hides rows, does not delete). We-do: filter to the top ten.
3. (25–30) I-do — three new functions. = COUNTIF(range, ">10") counts by a rule; = AVERAGE finds the mean; = IF(B2>10, "Hooray!", "Too low") shows one of two messages.
4. (30–50) You-do — practise on a chosen data set (Olympics / Premier League / YouTube): sort, filter, COUNTIF, AVERAGE, IF. Keep a screenshot of a result (activity worksheet).
   • Support: tick what AVERAGE and COUNTIF do.  • Core: write an AVERAGE function; say how COUNTIF differs from COUNTA.  • Challenge: write a full IF formula and check it against sorted data.
5. (50–55) Plenary — two stars and a wish; self-tick the four “I can…”. TA notes who used IF correctly.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

lessons.title: Assessment — conditional formatting & end-of-unit assessment
lessons.objectives:
I can use conditional formatting in a spreadsheet
I can sort and filter data
I can use functions to analyse data
I can make a chart from data
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: conditional formatting. TA: each pupil has their own copy of the forestry assessment spreadsheet. Read the “I can…” aloud. This lesson ends with the unit assessment.
1. (0–10) Optional starter — highlight by hand (starter worksheet). Pupils colour numbers over 20 green by hand; CHOOSE that doing this automatically by a rule is “conditional formatting”.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — conditional formatting. Set a rule (e.g. “greater than 10,000”) using the single-colour option and a predefined rule; the spreadsheet colours matching cells. Model one rule on the demo data.
   • Support: tick that conditional formatting changes a cell’s colour by a rule.  • Core: write AVERAGE/COUNTIF functions for the data.  • Challenge: write an IF formula and an hectares→acres formula.
3. (25–55) Summative assessment — forestry data (assessment worksheet). Pupils sort and filter, use functions (MAX/MIN/SUM/AVERAGE/COUNTIF), use IF, apply conditional formatting, and make a pie chart, working in their own copy. Keep screenshots of the sorted, analysed and formatted data.
   Mark with the answers spreadsheet. Support = the worksheet’s tick questions; Challenge = the written formulas. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
4. Plenary — self-tick the “I can…”; collect screenshots/spreadsheets as assessment evidence.
Differentiation on the SAME assessment task — every pupil works the same forestry data; nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.
